title: Throwing off the hump
subtitle: Making multiple small forms from a single centred mound of clay
excerpt: Throwing off the hump is a production technique in which a large mound of clay is centred once and multiple small forms are thrown from the top, one after another. Each finished form is cut from the mound and the next form begins. The method saves time when throwing a run of identical small cups, bowls, or egg cups. Requires a potter's wheel and kiln access.
sourceNotes: Off-the-hump throwing is a traditional production throwing method. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Settling a clay mound on the wheel head so it rotates without wobbling.
Pressing a hole into the centre of a centred mound to start a thrown pot.
Drawing clay upward between fingers to thin and raise the wall of a thrown pot.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim.
The first firing, around cone 06 (1000°C), that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware.
A pyrometric measure of heat-work. Cone 06 is roughly 1000°C; cone 6 is roughly 1220°C.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C.
Throwing off the hump is how production potters make a run of 20 or 30 small cups from a single session at the wheel. A 1-2 kg ball of clay is
centring

once. The top portion is then isolated and thrown into a form; the completed form is cut free from the hump with a wire tool and set to dry, and the next portion is drawn up from the mound. The technique requires a stable, well-centred hump. If the hump wobbles, each thrown form will be off-centre.
When to use it

Off-the-hump throwing suits forms up to about 250 g finished weight. Small cups, egg cups, small bowls, oil dipping dishes, and espresso cups are all good candidates. Larger forms are harder to control because pulling a tall wall off the hump allows the hump itself to wobble and the form becomes off-centre.
What you need
Smooth stoneware, 1-2 kg, wedged and free of air bubbles
Electric potter's wheel
Wire cutter
Needle tool
Pottery sponge and water bucket
Wooden boards for setting finished forms to dry
Step-by-step

Attach the full 1-2 kg ball to the wheel head and centre it with the same technique used for a full ball. The hump must be centred precisely before throwing begins. Even a small wobble is amplified in each form thrown above.

Isolate the top portion of clay for the first form by grasping the top of the centred mound with both hands and drawing upward to raise a column above the main mass. For a 150 g cup, draw up a column about 80-90 mm tall from the top of the hump.
Compress the top of the column with a palm to create a flat surface, then proceed with
opening
and
pulling a wall
as for a standard thrown cup, using only the isolated column of clay above the hump. The hump below acts as the support.
When the form is thrown, level the base of the cup where it meets the hump by pressing the wooden rib against the outside base at an angle. The base should sit about 10 mm above the hump surface.
Hold the wire cutter taut between both hands and slide it flat against the hump surface, cutting the base of the cup free. Slide the cut cup off the hump onto a wooden board to dry.

Re-wet the top of the hump with the sponge, draw up the next column, and repeat. Continue until the hump is reduced to a low mound, then use the remaining clay for one final larger form or wedge it back into the main clay supply.
Drying and firing
Forms cut from the hump dry with a rough base from the wire cut. Trim the base at the
leather-hard
stage with the loop tool to even the base thickness.
Bisque
at
cone
06, glaze, and fire to maturity.
What it looks like when it is working

A well-thrown set of hump cups will be consistent in size within about 5 mm of each other. The forms will stand upright without leaning after wire-cutting. The inside surfaces will be smooth and even without spiral ribbing. The base wire cut will be clean and horizontal, not angled.
Common mistakes
Hump wobbles: this happens when the original centring was not precise, or when the column was drawn up too quickly. Re-centre before pulling each column.
Wire cut goes through the form: the wire was held at an angle rather than flat. Hold the wire horizontal and press it along the hump surface before drawing it inward.
Forms are inconsistent sizes: caused by drawing up different column heights. Measure the column height before opening each form to keep the run consistent.
